name: Kang, Sang Hyeon
recent_1: Kim, J., Kang, S. H., Li, J., Mirka, G. A., & Dorneich, M. C. (2024). Effects of a passive back-support exosuit on postural control and cognitive performance during a fatigue-inducing posture maintenance task. Human Factors, 66(11), 2451–2467. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231221890
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated whether a passive back-support exosuit affected postural stability and cognitive task performance during a 16-minute static trunk flexion task that induced erector spinae fatigue. The exosuit improved postural control (reduced center-of-pressure sway) and enhanced performance on a dual-task information-integration cognitive measure, suggesting exosuit benefits extend beyond muscle unloading to support concurrent cognitive demands in occupational settings.
recent_2: Kang, S. H., Lynch, L., Wolf, E., & Mirka, G. A. (2024). Quantifying the effectiveness of a passive trunk-support exosuit at reducing erector spinae muscle fatigue during a quasi-static posture maintenance task. Ergonomics, 67(9), 1257–1266. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2295214
recent_2_synopsis: Participants maintained an 18-minute quasi-static trunk flexion posture with and without a passive back-support exosuit while EMG-derived fatigue indicators (median frequency, RMS) were tracked. The exosuit significantly attenuated erector spinae fatigue accumulation, providing quantitative evidence that passive trunk-support devices can extend sustainable working durations for jobs requiring sustained forward bending.
recent_3: Kang, S. H., & Mirka, G. A. (2023). Creep deformation of viscoelastic lumbar tissue during sustained submaximal trunk flexion postures. Journal of Biomechanics, 155, 111647. DOI: 10.1016/j.jbiomech.2023.111647
recent_3_synopsis: This paper characterized how viscoelastic lumbar soft tissues (ligaments, discs) creep — progressively deform under sustained load — during prolonged sub-maximal trunk flexion, linking exposure duration and posture magnitude to tissue deformation trajectories. The findings are foundational for understanding cumulative spinal loading in occupational contexts and for setting evidence-based guidelines on work-rest schedules involving sustained bending.
seminal_1: Kang, S. H., & Mirka, G. A. (2023). Effect of trunk flexion angle and time on lumbar and abdominal muscle activity while wearing a passive back-support exosuit device during simple posture-maintenance tasks. Ergonomics, 66(12), 2182–2192. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2191908
seminal_1_synopsis: This is Kang's most-cited work (~37 citations as of mid-2026), systematically examining how trunk flexion angle and exposure duration interact to shape lumbar and abdominal EMG patterns while wearing a passive exosuit. By documenting the angle- and time-dependent nature of exosuit effectiveness, the study established a key empirical foundation for understanding when and how much passive back-support devices reduce trunk muscle demand, influencing subsequent exosuit design and evaluation research.
seminal_2: Kang, S. H., Lee, J., & Jin, S. (2021). Effect of standing desk use on cognitive performance and physical workload while engaged with high cognitive demand tasks. Applied Ergonomics, 92, 103306. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2020.103306
seminal_2_synopsis: This study (~30 citations as of mid-2026) investigated whether standing desk use, compared with seated work, impaired cognitive performance or altered physical workload during demanding cognitive tasks. Results showed standing desks did not degrade cognitive performance but did increase physical workload indicators (discomfort, postural sway), providing nuanced evidence for the physical-cognitive trade-offs important to office ergonomics guidelines.
expertise_overview: Sang Hyeon Kang's research sits at the intersection of occupational biomechanics, spine ergonomics, and human performance. His primary focus is evaluating passive back-support exosuits and exoskeletons: he uses surface electromyography (EMG), postural sway analysis, and biomechanical modeling to quantify how these wearable assistive devices modulate trunk muscle activity, spinal load transfer, and fatigue during sustained and asymmetric posture-maintenance tasks representative of industrial and agricultural work. A parallel line of inquiry examines viscoelastic creep of lumbar passive tissues (ligaments, intervertebral discs) under prolonged flexion, with implications for work-rest schedule design. He also investigates how standing desks and workstation modifications affect the interplay between physical workload and cognitive performance, reflecting a broader interest in dual physical-cognitive demands in office and manual work settings. Methodologically, Kang combines controlled laboratory experiments, EMG-assisted biomechanical modeling, and machine learning approaches to fatigue prediction. His work is directly applicable to ergonomic intervention design, wearable technology assessment, and occupational health policy.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Anchor papers (four Human Factors DOIs) match exosuit/ergonomics profile exactly. Current affiliation: Assistant Professor, Department of Industrial and Entrepreneurial Engineering and Engineering Management, Western Michigan University, Kalamazoo, MI (previously Iowa State University PhD). Google Scholar profile: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=FhRtQZwAAAAJ. ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Sang-Hyeon-Kang. Citation counts sourced from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17. All six DOIs cited (recent_1 through seminal_2) were verified via Crossref works API (HTTP 200, title match) on 2026-06-17. No unverifiable citations.
